text: His career FTr+ is 165 — which means he draws 65% more FTs per FGA than the average player. For reference MJ was 110 (114 as a Bull). MJ only drew 14% more FTs per FGA than the average player during his career as a Bull. Kobe and LeBron had much better whistles, although nowhere near SGA level.
label: analysis
notes: FTr+ stat comparison across multiple players

text: He turns the ball over incredibly rarely for how much he has the rock. Especially in this day and age where 4 TOs for your primary superstar ball handler is the norm.

text: He's actually 13th in FTA/FGA while leading the league in drives, where players get fouled more.

text: From a skill perspective, you can't deny that he is one of the best in the game right now. text: Steven Adams orb%: 2014-2017 = 12.2% to 14.1%. 2018-2021 = 14.0% to 16.6%. 2022 = 17.9%. 2023 = 20.1%. 2025 = 21.8%. 2026 = 22.3%. The numbers match Adams' theory. As the older generation of centers left the league and new centers entered, Adams' offensive rebound rate skyrocketed.

text: Uber tall guys are rarely generational rebounders if ever — center of gravity is a bitch. Most rebounding records post-60s go to guys like Moses Malone, Barkley, Howard, even young Kevin Love. Those dudes were all 6'5-6'10 and no taller.

text: That Mitchell Robinson offensive rebound to put the series away was ridiculous. Wemby had position and was staring right at Mitch, but Mitch just walked him down under the rim with ease. Wemby's gotta get his weight up. He can take advantage of similarly built guys like Chet who have zero ability to move him around, but thicker 7-footers are able to move him at will.
label: analysis

text: It's a difficult balance — put on weight and he loses mobility and increases his risks of injury. What he needs is to improve his technique. Get his base wider, his center of gravity lower. He should model his game after Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, who was quite skinny too.

notes: Tactical prescription for Wemby with historical comparator

text: He'd do well to model his rebounding off Kevin Garnett. He was always skinny and weighed a lot less than many centers but knew about positioning and boxing out as well as anyone. KG had super wiry strength and tree trunk legs and a crazy strong base by the end.
label: analysis

text: It's not as clearcut as saying today's players are 'more skilled.' Players are better from 3 but most have poor or no midrange game. A lot of bigs today can't box out, have poor post moves. text: Our starting 5 of John Wall, Bradley Beal, Otto Porter, Nene, Gortat worked so beautifully. I believe in 2016 (Markieff Morris replacing Nene) was statistically the most effective starting lineup, even better than the Warriors. Too bad our bench sucked and would get outscored every game.

text: Brook Lopez will always be my king of boxing out. text: You don't even have to be THAT big. Tatum averaged 10 rebs a game this season purely off good positioning and boxing out. He's been improving at it every year — despite all the other shit he does, he's still down there fighting for boards.

text: That's because Wemby is quite young and quite tall. He has never had to compete with guys of similar size. Not a single coach probably told him he needed to position better and box out, because with his length alone he was getting an adequate number of rebounds his whole life.

text: Skills of all positions have been swapped out and some have been lost to time. The average center doesn't deploy flip shots or drop steps either. Proper post passes are not standardized guard skills today. The game evolved but certain baseline fundamentals disappeared with it.
